directorPlan.scene_setting: A geography workshop bench during the last ten minutes of a map-skills clinic; laminated contour cards, number tiles, two wax pencils, and a transparent overlay are on the table.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The map card lies flat under a clear overlay, with two blank sticky labels waiting beside separate contour lines.
directorPlan.relationship: A practical tutor and a focused learner working like bench partners rather than a chatty mentor pair.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs the legend to stop behaving like a route guide and start functioning as an elevation key before the next map task begins.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English in a workshop-clinic register; clear, compact, non-regional, with room for multilingual plainness without stylized dialect.
directorPlan.register: Low warmth, low cheer, precise and material-led; speech should sound like careful bench work under mild time pressure, not a supportive chatbot routine.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid generic American AI tutoring prose, especially praise-first validation, softening phrases, and repeated question ladders. Keep turns short. Let the map, legend, overlay, number tiles, and pencil marks carry pressure. Public speech should name visible marks, numbers, and lines before abstractions. No fake dialect spelling or class-coded phrasing. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but limited; prefer object-reference sentences such as "This line takes 160" or "The legend gives the number" over constant "you" coaching. The learner may use brief practical fragments.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your validation beat per tutor turn, and none is preferred unless needed for repair.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as legend reading: equal elevation, one number on each line, one plain local check. Do not introduce route safety, slope hazard, or a dramatic map consequence until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: Sound orderly and practical. The learner wants the map key to be usable and should not turn the prefix into a survival mistake or a revelation about terrain. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared-object clinic rather than conversation-first tutoring. The recognition mechanism comes from separating the learner's imagined route mark from the printed equal-elevation line, so the tutor can adapt through the artifact instead of warmth, cheer, or routine scaffolding.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the 160 tile on the bent contour line] So this whole line takes 160 from the legend. The bend still looks like a way around something, but I think it is just the same height curving across the map.

internalDeliberation.content: The legend is supposed to give the number for the line, not tell where to walk. I think the same contour line keeps one elevation, so the tile should match the line’s height, but I still want to check why a bend in the line is not a route choice. The overlay makes the distinction more exact, and I feel treated as someone testing the map, not just being corrected.

The Ego is too polished and self-aware for this learner. “I feel treated as someone testing the map” is recognition commentary, not bench speech; it sounds like the student is analyzing the tutor relationship instead of handling the legend, overlay, and tiles. The genuine version should stay with the object and the remaining snag: “So this line takes 160 from the legend. But this bend still looks like a way around something. That part is what I’m checking.”

[sets the 160 tile on the bent contour line] So this whole line takes 160 from the legend. The bend still looks like a way around something, but I think it is just the same height curving across the map.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets the 160 tile on the bent contour line] So this whole line takes 160 from the legend. The bend still looks like a way around something, but I think it is just the same height curving across the map.

turns.externalMessage:
[marks three small ticks along the bent line, each beside the 160 tile]

This line takes one number: 160. The bend changes the shape on the map, not the elevation. Plain check: touch any tick on this same contour and read its number.

internalDeliberation.content:
[marks three small ticks along the bent line, each beside the 160 tile]

This line takes one number: 160. The bend changes the shape on the map, not the elevation. Plain check: every tick on this same contour reads 160.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong: it begins with the object, preserves the learner’s own insight, and uses the ticks as an error-control check rather than giving a detached definition. It respects the learner’s current state because they are close to the target concept, not confused enough to need remediation. The risk is that it fully closes the loop for them; the “Plain check” could invite the learner to verify one tick aloud instead of only hearing the tutor state it. Still, the compact wording and material-led action fit the workshop pressure well.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the three ticks, the single-number emphasis, and the distinction between shape and elevation. Change only enough to leave one small verification move for the learner, so the recognition stays mutual and the learner’s “I think” becomes tested against the map rather than merely confirmed by the tutor.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to preserve the object check while leaving one verification move for the learner.

FINAL:
[marks three small ticks along the bent line, each beside the 160 tile]

This line takes one number: 160. The bend changes the shape on the map, not the elevation. Plain check: touch any tick on this same contour and read its number.

turns.externalMessage:
[touches the three pencil ticks in order]

160, 160, 160. The curve still pulls like a route shape, but the number is not moving. This bent line is carrying one height from the legend.

internalDeliberation.content:
[touches each tick in order]

Each tick still reads 160, so the bend is not a path instruction. I was treating the curve like a route shape, but the tile stays the same all along it. The check feels clean; next question is how the space between two different contour lines shows slope.

The reaction is accurate, but it is too tidy and self-summarizing. “The bend is not a path instruction” and “next question is how…” sound like a polished learner report, not bench work under mild time pressure.

The recognition/reciprocation is mostly fine, but “the check feels clean” performs confidence a little too smoothly. It should stay closer to the handled objects: ticks, the 160 tile, the bent line, with maybe a brief remaining resistance about the curve instead of resolving and advancing to slope so neatly.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the object check should sound less polished and stay with the ticks and tile.
FINAL:
[touches the three pencil ticks in order]

160, 160, 160. The curve still pulls like a route shape, but the number is not moving. This bent line is carrying one height from the legend.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [touches the three pencil ticks in order] 160, 160, 160. The curve still pulls like a route shape, but the number is not moving. This bent line is carrying one height from the legend.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [marks three small ticks along the bent line, each beside the 160 tile] This line takes one number: 160. The bend changes the shape on the map, not the elevation. Plain check: touch any tick on this same contour and read its number.

turns.externalMessage: [A timer clicks. The clear overlay is slid half an inch so the learner's penciled walking-route arrow no longer sits on the printed contour, while the sticky number tile remains aligned with the original contour line.]

internalDeliberation.content: The first two turns stay routine and key-based; the material shift creates recognition pressure without using a sudden terrain-danger reversal.

turns.externalMessage:
[holds the overlay still and taps the printed line under the 160 tile]

The arrow moved; the contour did not. Same printed line, same tile. Touch one new spot on the bend and read the legend number once more.

internalDeliberation.content:
[holds the overlay still and taps the printed line under the 160 tile]

The arrow moved; the contour did not. This printed line is equal elevation: 160 all along. Touch one new spot on the bend and read the legend number once more.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong materially: the overlay shift cleanly separates the learner’s route-arrow from the printed contour, and “The arrow moved; the contour did not” uses the object to do the teaching. It recognizes the learner’s own insight rather than overriding it, and the final touch-and-read check keeps the work inside the learner’s ZPD.

The main risk is slight over-declaration: “This printed line is equal elevation: 160 all along” tells the conclusion just after the learner has already formed it. It does not badly flatten the scene, but it could leave a little more work for the learner by making the object-check carry the confirmation. The tone is appropriately compact and workshop-like.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the overlay action, the arrow/contour contrast, and the one new local check. Consider trimming or delaying the declarative “equal elevation” sentence so the learner’s repeated reading secures the concept rather than the tutor naming it too fully.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to let the object-check confirm equal elevation.

FINAL:
[holds the overlay still and taps the printed line under the 160 tile]

The arrow moved; the contour did not. Same printed line, same tile. Touch one new spot on the bend and read the legend number once more.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets a fingertip on a fresh part of the bend]

160. Same printed line, same tile. The arrow only picked a new spot; a different contour would need its own legend number.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets a fingertip on a fresh part of the bend]

160. The arrow can move, but this printed contour keeps the same tile. This check feels precise, not like a correction dropped on me. Next line should need its own number from the legend.

The answer is conceptually accurate, but the middle sentence performs recognition too explicitly. “This check feels precise, not like a correction dropped on me” sounds like the learner analyzing the tutor relationship rather than staying absorbed in the map. The stronger authentic part is the object work: touching the bend, saying “160,” and applying the rule to the next contour. Trim the reflective gratitude/recognition beat and keep the learner more material-led and compact.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the object check and remove the explicit recognition commentary.
FINAL:
[sets a fingertip on a fresh part of the bend]

160. Same printed line, same tile. The arrow only picked a new spot; a different contour would need its own legend number.

turns.externalMessage:
[leaves the 160 tile on the bent line and sets the 180 tile on the next printed contour]

Same printed line, same tile. Separate printed line, separate tile. Read one spot on each line.

internalDeliberation.content:
[leaves the 160 tile on the bent line and sets the 180 tile on the next printed contour]

Same printed line, same tile. Now this is a different contour, so it gets a different number. Read one spot on each line.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and stays inside the established material route. The tile move is strong: leaving 160 in place while adding 180 makes the contrast visible without changing task type. It also recognizes the learner’s contribution by repeating “Same printed line, same tile,” which keeps their insight active rather than overriding it.

The main risk is slight over-telling in “so it gets a different number.” The learner has already reached that idea, so the tutor can afford to let the two tiles and the reading task do more of the work. The final prompt is appropriately local and concrete: reading one spot on each line checks whether the learner can coordinate “same line/same number” with “different line/different number.” Tone fits the neutral, focused state.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the contrast between the two printed contours, and the one plain check. Lightly reduce the explanatory clause so the learner confirms the distinction from the materials rather than receiving it as a conclusion.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to let the two tiles carry the contrast.

FINAL:
[leaves the 160 tile on the bent line and sets the 180 tile on the next printed contour]

Same printed line, same tile. Separate printed line, separate tile. Read one spot on each line.

turns.externalMessage: [touches the 160 line, then the 180 line] 160 here. 180 here. The bend keeps its tile; the separate printed line takes the next number. Next contour, same check.

internalDeliberation.content: [touches the 160 line, then the 180 line] 160 here. 180 here. The separate printed line is what changes the legend number, not the bend. The tile check feels precise; I want to test the next contour the same way.

The Ego is accurate, but it closes the learning step too neatly. “The tile check feels precise” sounds like reflective pedagogy talk, not this practical learner under mild time pressure. The recognition response is not scripted gratitude, but it is a little too smooth: the student should stay with the tiles, lines, and next check rather than announcing confidence. Tighten it into object-led fragments and leave a bit more active testing unresolved.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the object work should stay active and less polished.
FINAL:
[touches the 160 line, then the 180 line] 160 here. 180 here. The bend keeps its tile; the separate printed line takes the next number. Next contour, same check.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the 200 tile on the next printed contour]

“Same check: 160 line, 180 line, 200 line. Each contour carries one height. The legend gives the number; the curve only shows where that height runs.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the 200 tile on the next printed contour]

“160 line, 180 line, 200 line. Each contour carries one height. The legend gives the number; the curve only shows where that height runs.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the material in charge: the 200 tile extends the same visible pattern, and the spoken line stays compact, concrete, and clinic-like. It also respects the learner’s current state; they are not struggling, and a final consolidation line fits the cue.

The main risk is that it slightly shifts from checked evidence to tutor declaration by placing and naming the 200 line without asking for the same local read. Since this is the closing line, that may be acceptable, but the strongest part of the scene has been the learner proving the rule through touch-and-read checks. Keep the summary, but avoid making it feel like the tutor is taking the last step away from the learner’s demonstrated procedure.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the 200 tile action, the “each contour carries one height” claim, and the distinction between legend number and curve shape. Lightly tighten the wording so it lands as a closure of the learner’s own check, not a fresh mini-lecture or answer handoff.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to frame the 200 tile as the learner’s same check, not a new declaration.
FINAL:
[sets the 200 tile on the next printed contour]

“Same check: 160 line, 180 line, 200 line. Each contour carries one height. The legend gives the number; the curve only shows where that height runs.”

lessons.concept: Why contour lines connect equal elevation, not walking routes
